# ADR-260015: Visualization and Interaction Model for Graph Workspace MVP

## Workflow

- This ADR records accepted D1-D7 visualization and interaction decisions for MVP delivery.
- The decision is accepted because interaction semantics and performance targets directly constrain implementation strategy and release readiness.

Status: Accepted
Date: 2026-04-17
Owners: Repository owner, product collaborator

## Context

The product is already committed to graph-first interaction, full workspace navigation, and side-panel editing.

The project now needs explicit decisions on visualization modes, interaction primitives, creation/deletion safety, performance expectations, and editing semantics to avoid inconsistent implementation choices.

## Decision

- D1 accepted decision:
  - MVP uses one primary graph canvas with focused subgraph context via filtering within the same renderer.
  - Multiple distinct graph modes are deferred.
- D2 accepted decision:
  - View switching stays inside the workspace using tabs/panels/context controls, not route-level page transitions.
- D3 accepted decision:
  - MVP primitives include selection, atom drag positioning, drag-to-connect bond creation, pan, zoom, search focus/center, and delete actions.
  - Advanced multi-select/lasso and richer shortcut suites are deferred.
- D4 accepted decision:
  - Creation flow is canvas-first: quick-add in canvas context, then complete details in side panel.
  - Bond naming occurs as immediate inline/panel follow-up after connect.
- D5 accepted decision:
  - Deletion is hard-delete with safety controls: atom delete confirmation, undo window for atom/bond delete, no bulk delete in MVP.
- D6 accepted decision:
  - MVP performance targets approximately 300-500 atoms and 600-1000 bonds per workspace, perceived interactive response under 100 ms, and typical bootstrap-search render under 2 seconds.
- D7 accepted decision:
  - Spatial/relationship operations are direct manipulation in canvas.
  - Metadata-heavy or validation-heavy edits are handled in side panel/forms.

## Alternatives Considered

- Option A: Single canvas mode with progressive interaction primitives.
- Option B: Multiple visualization modes and full advanced interaction tooling in MVP.
- Option C: Form-first creation/editing with minimal direct manipulation.

## Consequences

- Positive:
  - Preserves graph-first value while limiting MVP complexity.
  - Reduces context switching and keeps editing flows predictable.
  - Provides measurable interaction/performance acceptance thresholds.
- Negative:
  - Advanced power-user interactions are deferred.
  - Hard-delete model still needs careful undo UX to prevent accidental loss.
- Risks:
  - Performance may degrade near upper dataset bounds if rendering and layout are not optimized.

## Verification

- End-to-end checks confirm required direct-manipulation primitives and canvas-first creation flow.
- Safety checks confirm atom-delete confirmation and undo behavior for atom/bond deletion.
- Performance checks confirm dataset and latency thresholds under representative MVP conditions.
- UX checks confirm no route-level context switches are required for core workspace tasks.

## Rollback

- Supersede this ADR if usability/performance evidence shows required interaction model changes.
- Update linked requirements and backlog items to the superseding decision.
